item.title: About
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Your Trusted Cybersecurity Partner

ShieldNet MSP was founded with a single mission: to bring enterprise-grade cybersecurity to small and mid-sized businesses. We believe every organization deserves the same level of protection that Fortune 500 companies enjoy — without the Fortune 500 price tag.

Why Choose ShieldNet?

  • 24/7 Security Operations Center (SOC) monitoring
  • Certified team: CISSP, CEH, CompTIA Security+
  • Average incident response time under 15 minutes
  • 99.97% uptime SLA guarantee
  • Compliance-first approach (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC 2)

Our Story

After a decade of building security programs for enterprises, our founders saw the gap: SMBs were being hit hardest by cyberattacks yet had the fewest resources to defend against them. ShieldNet was built to close that gap — delivering managed security that scales with your business.

item.title: Services
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Comprehensive Managed Security Services

Email Security & DMARC

Protect your domain from phishing and spoofing with our full DMARC implementation service. We configure SPF, DKIM, and DMARC policies, monitor reports, and progressively enforce reject policies — stopping attackers from impersonating your brand.

Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR)

Next-gen antivirus is not enough. Our EDR solution provides real-time behavioral analysis, automated threat containment, and forensic investigation capabilities across all your endpoints.

24/7 Security Monitoring

Our SOC team monitors your environment around the clock. We correlate logs from firewalls, endpoints, cloud services, and email to detect threats before they become breaches.

Vulnerability Management

Regular vulnerability scans, patch management, and remediation tracking. We prioritize by real-world exploitability, not just CVSS scores, so your team fixes what matters first.

Security Awareness Training

Your employees are your first line of defense. Our training program includes simulated phishing campaigns, interactive modules, and compliance tracking dashboards.

Incident Response

When a breach happens, every minute counts. Our IR team is on standby 24/7 with documented playbooks for ransomware, data exfiltration, BEC, and more.

item.title: Contact
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Get Your Free Security Assessment

Ready to find out where your business stands? Our team will perform a complimentary security assessment covering email authentication, endpoint protection, network exposure, and compliance gaps.

What to Expect

  1. 30-minute discovery call to understand your environment
  2. Non-invasive external vulnerability scan
  3. DMARC/SPF/DKIM configuration review
  4. Executive summary with prioritized recommendations

Phone: (555) 012-3456
Email: info@shieldnet-demo.com
Hours: SOC: 24/7 | Office: Mon–Fri, 8am–6pm EST

item.title: Why DMARC Is No Longer Optional for Your Business
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Email remains the number one attack vector for cybercriminals. Business Email Compromise (BEC) cost organizations $2.7 billion in 2023 alone. Yet most small businesses still haven't implemented DMARC — the protocol specifically designed to prevent email spoofing.

What Is DMARC?

DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance) builds on SPF and DKIM to give domain owners control over what happens when authentication fails. You can monitor, quarantine, or reject unauthenticated emails — and get detailed reports about who's sending mail on your behalf.

The Three-Phase Approach

We recommend a phased DMARC rollout:

  1. Monitor (p=none): Collect data without affecting mail flow. Identify all legitimate sending sources.
  2. Quarantine (p=quarantine): Route suspicious emails to spam. Verify no legitimate mail is affected.
  3. Reject (p=reject): Block all unauthenticated emails. Full protection achieved.

The Business Case

Beyond security, DMARC improves email deliverability. Gmail and Microsoft now prioritize DMARC-authenticated domains, meaning your marketing emails are more likely to reach inboxes instead of spam folders.

item.title: The Anatomy of a Ransomware Attack: What SMBs Need to Know
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Ransomware attacks against small businesses increased 150% in the past year. The average ransom demand for SMBs now exceeds $170,000, and that doesn't include downtime costs, data recovery, and reputational damage.

How Ransomware Gets In

The most common entry points are:

  • Phishing emails with malicious attachments or links (67% of cases)
  • Exposed RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol) with weak credentials (20%)
  • Unpatched vulnerabilities in public-facing applications (10%)

The Kill Chain

Modern ransomware doesn't just encrypt files. Attackers now follow a methodical process: initial access, privilege escalation, lateral movement, data exfiltration, and finally encryption. The entire process can take weeks, giving defenders multiple opportunities to detect and stop the attack.

Defense in Depth

No single product stops ransomware. Effective defense requires layered security: email filtering, endpoint detection, network segmentation, offline backups, and trained employees who recognize phishing attempts.

item.title: Zero Trust Architecture: Beyond the Buzzword
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Zero Trust has become the most overused term in cybersecurity marketing. But beneath the hype lies a genuinely transformative security model — one that's increasingly essential as remote work dissolves traditional network perimeters.

The Core Principle

"Never trust, always verify." Every access request is treated as if it originates from an untrusted network, regardless of whether the user is inside or outside the corporate perimeter.

Practical Implementation for SMBs

  • MFA everywhere: Multi-factor authentication on all accounts, no exceptions
  • Least privilege: Users get minimum access needed for their role
  • Microsegmentation: Network segments that limit lateral movement
  • Continuous verification: Device health checks and behavioral analytics

Start Small

You don't need to rebuild your entire infrastructure. Start with MFA, implement conditional access policies, and gradually segment your network. The goal is progress, not perfection.

item.title: HIPAA Compliance Checklist for Small Healthcare Practices
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
If your practice handles patient health information, HIPAA compliance isn't optional — it's the law. Yet many small practices struggle with the technical requirements, risking fines up to $1.5 million per violation category.

The Security Rule Essentials

  1. Risk Assessment: Document threats, vulnerabilities, and the likelihood of exploitation. This is the foundation of your entire compliance program.
  2. Access Controls: Unique user IDs, automatic logoff, encryption of PHI at rest and in transit.
  3. Audit Logs: Record who accessed what, when, and from where. Retain logs for at least 6 years.
  4. Backup & Recovery: Regular backups with tested restoration procedures. Ransomware can be a HIPAA breach if backups fail.
  5. Training: Annual security awareness training for all workforce members who handle PHI.

Business Associate Agreements

Every vendor that touches your patient data needs a BAA. This includes your cloud provider, email service, billing software, and IT support company. No BAA means no compliance.

item.title: SPF, DKIM, and DMARC: The Email Authentication Trinity Explained
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Email authentication is a three-legged stool. SPF, DKIM, and DMARC each play a distinct role, and you need all three for comprehensive protection against email spoofing.

SPF (Sender Policy Framework)

SPF lets you declare which IP addresses are authorized to send email for your domain. It's a DNS TXT record listing your legitimate mail servers. When a receiving server gets an email, it checks the sender's IP against your SPF record.

DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)

DKIM adds a cryptographic signature to outgoing emails. The receiving server verifies this signature against a public key published in your DNS. Unlike SPF, DKIM survives email forwarding.

DMARC: The Policy Layer

DMARC ties SPF and DKIM together with a policy: what should receivers do when authentication fails? It also provides reporting, so you can see exactly who's sending email using your domain — both legitimate services and attackers.

Implementation Order

Always implement in this order: SPF first, DKIM second, DMARC third. Each layer builds on the previous one. Rushing to DMARC p=reject without proper SPF and DKIM alignment will break your legitimate email.

item.title: 5 Network Security Mistakes That Leave SMBs Vulnerable
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
After hundreds of security assessments, we see the same mistakes repeated across small businesses. These aren't exotic vulnerabilities — they're basic misconfigurations that attackers exploit daily.

1. Default Credentials

Routers, switches, printers, and IoT devices often ship with default admin passwords. Attackers scan for these automatically. Change every default credential on your network.

2. Flat Network Architecture

When every device is on the same network segment, a compromised workstation can reach your servers, printers, and IoT devices directly. Segment your network by function and sensitivity.

3. No DNS Filtering

DNS filtering blocks connections to known malicious domains before any data is exchanged. It's one of the cheapest, most effective security controls available.

4. Unmonitored Remote Access

RDP exposed to the internet is an invitation for brute-force attacks. Use a VPN with MFA, and monitor all remote sessions with logging and session recording.

5. No Patch Management

Known vulnerabilities with public exploits remain unpatched for an average of 60 days in SMB environments. Automate patching for operating systems and third-party applications.
